evidence: We are incorporating AI solutions into our business, products, services and features, and we are leveraging AI, including generative AI, machine learning and similar tools and technologies, in our product development, operations and software programming.
The rapid evolution of AI...will require significant resources to develop, test and maintain our platforms, offerings, services and features to implement AI ethically.
We are leveraging AI, including generative AI, machine learning and similar tools and technologies, in our product development, operations and software programming.
We are incorporating AI solutions into our business, products, services and features.

The use of AI in the development of our products and services could cause loss or theft of intellectual property, as well as subject us to risks related to intellectual property infringement or misappropriation, data privacy and cybersecurity.
If the AI tools that we use are deficient, inaccurate or controversial, we could incur operational inefficiencies, competitive harm, legal liability, brand or reputational harm.
Our personnel could, unbeknownst to us, improperly utilize AI and machine learning-technology while carrying out their responsibilities.

limitations: No quantified AI-specific investment amounts, headcount, or capex figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 (named but unquantified).
AI use cases named (product development, operations, software programming) but no deployment metrics or quantified outcomes disclosed; concreteness capped at 3.

Risk disclosure is company-specific with named mechanisms (IP loss, cybersecurity, discriminatory outputs, personnel misuse, regulatory evolution) but no quantification or named governance body, capping risk score at 4.

Data center revenue growth discussed extensively but is attributable to power generation products sold to data centers, not AI investment by Cummins itself; excluded from ai_investment_intensity per anti-inflation rule.
